# UC-22.2.6 · NIS2 Risk Analysis and Information System Security Policy Evidence

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Risk, Compliance &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We keep an eye on your risk register and official security rulebooks so overdue reviews and half-finished approvals show up in one list. That way the people responsible can fix paperwork before it becomes a crisis.*

---

## Description

Joins normalized risk-register events and ServiceNow policy rows to a static policy and cadence table, so open high scores past due reviews, stale approvals, and non-approved policy drafts appear as one auditor-facing queue.

## Value

Risk and policy teams spend less time reconciling three spreadsheets before board meetings because the same search proves reviews ran, documents who owns each line, and shows where acceptance or republishing slipped.

## Implementation

(1) Land `grc:risk` via TA pull or HEC with stable `risk_id`. (2) Enable `snow:sn_grc_policy` input with RBAC scoped to the information security policy catalog. (3) Maintain `nis2_is_security_policies.csv` from your GRC tool of record. (4) Schedule weekly; route to the risk committee digest channel. (5) Sign exports per `evidenceSigning`.

## Evidence

Scheduled `UC-22.2.6` compliance report, signed CSV/PDF bundle, and chain-of-custody log referencing the RFC3161 token for each quarterly submission to the board pack.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Seed `index=grc` with `risk_id=RISK-9001`, `risk_score=80`, `risk_status=open`, `next_review_date` set three months in the past, and confirm the Monday schedule emails a row with `source_lane=risk_register`; seed `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` with `approval_status=draft` for `policy_number=POL-5001` and verify it appears with `source_lane=policy_record` while approved policies with fresh `last_approved` remain absent.

### Negative scenario

Move every in-scope risk to `risk_status=treated` with a future `next_review_date`, flip all policies to `approval_status=approved` inside the CSV `review_cycle_days` window, reload the knowledge bundle, and rerun ad-hoc to get zero rows even though historical drill data still exists in the indexes for traceability.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- This UC merges two separate compliance data streams into a single auditor-facing queue: (1) GRC risk register entries from `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk`, and (2) information security policy records from `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy`. The dual-lane architecture means you need both feeds healthy before the search produces meaningful results. If you only have one feed, the search will still run but will only surface breaches from that lane — which is acceptable during initial rollout but must be documented as a known gap.
- Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) ≥8.0 installed on a Heavy Forwarder or Search Head. The TA uses OAuth 2.0 with a dedicated integration user. In ServiceNow: navigate to System OAuth > Application Registry > Create an OAuth API endpoint for external clients. Record the Client ID and Client Secret. In Splunk: Apps > Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow > Configuration > Account, create a new account with the ServiceNow instance URL (e.g., `https://yourcompany.service-now.com`), client credentials, and a refresh token. The integration user must have `sn_grc.viewer` role for GRC policy tables and `itil` role for CMDB cross-references.
- ServiceNow GRC module: the `sn_grc_policy` table stores information security policies with fields `number` (policy_number), `name`, `state` (approval_status values: Draft, Pending Approval, Approved, Retired), `u_last_approved` (custom date field recording last formal approval), `u_framework` (the regulatory framework the policy supports — ISO 27001, NIS2, SOC 2, etc.), and `u_review_cycle_days` (the mandated review frequency). If your ServiceNow instance uses different field names, create FIELDALIAS entries in `props.conf` to map them to the expected names.
- GRC risk register data source: this can come from ServiceNow's `sn_grc_risk` table via the same TA, or from a third-party GRC tool (Archer, LogicGate, OneTrust, ServiceNow IRM) via HEC or scripted input. The critical fields are `risk_id` (unique identifier), `risk_title`, `risk_score` (numeric 0-100 scale, where higher = worse), `risk_status` (Open, Assessing, Treated, Accepted, Closed), `next_review_date` (ISO 8601), and `owner` (the risk owner's email or name). If your GRC tool uses a different scoring scale (e.g., 1-25 or Low/Medium/High/Critical), normalize to 0-100 in `props.conf` using a calculated field: `EVAL-risk_score = case(risk_rating="Critical", 90, risk_rating="High", 75, risk_rating="Medium", 50, risk_rating="Low", 25)`.
- CSV lookup `nis2_is_security_policies.csv` with columns: `policy_id` (matches `risk_id` for risk rows or `policy_number` for policy rows — this is the `record_key` in the SPL), `policy_name` (human-readable title), `approval_status` (expected status), `last_approved` (ISO 8601 date of last formal board/CISO approval), `review_cycle_days` (integer — typically 365 for annual review, 180 for semi-annual, 90 for quarterly). This lookup is the "truth table" — it defines what the cadence should be. The SPL compares actual ServiceNow/GRC data against this expectation. Store in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-grc/lookups/` and version-control in Git. Assign a named owner (typically the GRC analyst) who updates it after each governance committee meeting.
- CSV lookup `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv` with columns: `risk_id`, `exception_state` (approved, pending, expired), `suppress_until` (ISO 8601 date), `approver`, `justification`. This handles legitimate exceptions: risks formally accepted by the board, policies in planned revision cycles, etc. Without this register, every accepted risk will surface as a false positive.
- Index retention: `index=grc` ≥730 days (the SPL uses `earliest=-730d@d` to capture two full annual review cycles). `index=itsm` ≥365 days.
- RBAC: role `nis2_grc_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = grc, itsm, audit_evidence` and `srchFilter = (index=grc OR index=itsm)`. The GRC data may contain board-level risk appetite statements and policy drafts that are not appropriate for general SOC analysts.
- Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) is optional but recommended — it provides the asset framework for mapping `risk_object` to business services, and correlation search infrastructure for automated notable creation.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
**ServiceNow GRC policy feed:**
In the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow, navigate to Inputs > Create New Input > Table. Configure:
- Table name: `sn_grc_policy`
- Include columns: `number,name,state,u_last_approved,u_framework,u_review_cycle_days,sys_updated_on`
- Filter: `u_frameworkLIKEnis2 OR u_frameworkLIKEiso27001` (only pull policies relevant to your compliance scope)
- Interval: 3600 seconds (hourly — policies don't change frequently)
- Index: `itsm`
- Sourcetype: `snow:sn_grc_policy`

Verify the feed:
```
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(number) as policies dc(state) as states
| table count policies states
```
Expected: `policies` should match the count in ServiceNow's `sn_grc_policy` list view filtered to your framework scope. If `policies=0`, the modular input is not pulling data — check `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/ta_snow_ticket.log` for REST errors.

**Field alignment for ServiceNow:**
Create `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-grc/local/props.conf`:
```ini
[snow:sn_grc_policy]
FIELDALIAS-policy_number = number AS policy_number
FIELDALIAS-approval_status = state AS approval_status
FIELDALIAS-last_approved = u_last_approved AS last_approved
FIELDALIAS-review_cycle = u_review_cycle_days AS review_cycle_days
FIELDALIAS-framework = u_framework AS u_framework
```

**GRC risk register feed:**
If using ServiceNow IRM, add another table input for `sn_grc_risk`:
- Table name: `sn_grc_risk`
- Include columns: `number,short_description,u_risk_score,state,u_next_review_date,assigned_to`
- Interval: 3600
- Index: `grc`
- Sourcetype: `grc:risk`

Create field aliases:
```ini
[grc:risk]
FIELDALIAS-risk_id = number AS risk_id
FIELDALIAS-risk_title = short_description AS risk_title
FIELDALIAS-risk_score = u_risk_score AS risk_score
FIELDALIAS-risk_status = state AS risk_status
FIELDALIAS-next_review = u_next_review_date AS next_review_date
FIELDALIAS-owner = assigned_to AS owner
```

If using a third-party GRC tool (Archer, LogicGate), configure a scripted input or HEC endpoint. For HEC:
```ini
[http://grc_risk_feed]
token = <HEC-TOKEN>
index = grc
sourcetype = grc:risk
```
The upstream system must POST JSON with the required fields. Validate with: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-24h | stats count dc(risk_id) as risks`. If `risks=0`, check HEC token status and the upstream script.

**Lookup deployment:**
Place `nis2_is_security_policies.csv` and `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-grc/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_is_security_policies]
filename = nis2_is_security_policies.csv
case_sensitive_match = false

[nis2_risk_exception_register]
filename = nis2_risk_exception_register.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```
Smoke test: `| inputlookup nis2_is_security_policies.csv | stats count`. Expected: matches the number of rows in the CSV. If zero, the lookup definition or file path is wrong.

### Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.6 NIS2 Risk and Policy Evidence`.

#### Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

- **Dual-lane processing with `source_lane`**: the `OR` in the base search pulls both `grc:risk` and `snow:sn_grc_policy` events. The `eval source_lane=if(index="grc","risk_register","policy_record")` tags each event so downstream logic can apply lane-specific breach conditions. This is more efficient than running two separate searches and appending results.

- **`coalesce(risk_id, policy_number, u_policy_id)` for `record_key`**: GRC risk rows use `risk_id`, policy rows use `policy_number`, and some ServiceNow configurations use `u_policy_id`. `coalesce` picks the first non-null value, creating a universal join key for the lookup. If all three are null, the event is unkeyed and will not match the lookup — this is a data quality signal that should trigger investigation.

- **Four `review_breach` conditions (sequential `eval`)**: the SPL initializes `review_breach=0` then applies four independent tests:
  1. Risk register row where `next_review_date` has passed (`now() > next_epoch`). This catches overdue risk reviews.
  2. Risk register row where `risk_score ≥ 75` AND status is still open/new/assessing. This catches high-severity risks that have not been treated or accepted.
  3. Policy record where `approval_status` is draft/pending/rejected. This catches policies that have not completed the approval lifecycle.
  4. Policy record where `days_since_approval > review_cycle_days`. This catches approved policies that are overdue for re-review.
  Each condition sets `review_breach=1` independently — a single record can trip multiple conditions. The `where review_breach=1` filter at the end keeps only actionable rows.

- **`match(lower(risk_status), "open|new|assessing")`**: the `match()` function applies a regex against lowercase status values, accommodating different GRC tools that may use "Open", "NEW", or "Assessing" with varying capitalisation. The pipe-delimited pattern acts as an OR.

- **`stats latest()` aggregation**: after filtering to breaches, the `stats` command groups by `record_key` and `source_lane`, keeping only the most recent values. This deduplicates multiple events for the same risk/policy over the 730-day lookback window.

- **`sort 0 - risk_score`**: unbounded descending sort puts the highest-scoring risks at the top — these are the items most likely to be questioned by auditors or board members.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * 1` (every Monday at 06:00). Time range: `-730d@d to now`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) Email digest to the risk committee distribution list with subject line containing `[NIS2] Risk and Policy Gaps — <result_count> items`. (2) Output to lookup `nis2_weekly_risk_summary.csv` for executive dashboard consumption. (3) For items where `risk_score ≥ 90`, create a ServiceNow incident via the TA's alert action with assignment group `GRC-Risk-Committee`, priority `P2`.

### Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Risk register parity check**: select five risk IDs from the Splunk results. In your GRC tool's UI (ServiceNow > GRC > Risk > All Risks, filtered by the same IDs), verify:
- `risk_score` matches within ±1 point (rounding differences are acceptable)
- `risk_status` matches exactly
- `next_review_date` matches the date shown in the GRC tool
- If any field diverges, trace the issue: is it a field alias mismatch, a stale index, or a legitimate data lag from the TA's polling interval?

(b) **Policy approval parity check**: select three policy numbers from the results. In ServiceNow > GRC > Policy > Policies, verify:
- `approval_status` (state) matches. ServiceNow uses numeric states internally (1=Draft, 2=Pending Approval, 3=Approved, 4=Retired). The TA should translate these to text — if it doesn't, add `EVAL-approval_status = case(state=1,"draft",state=2,"pending",state=3,"approved",state=4,"retired")` in `props.conf`.
- `last_approved` (u_last_approved) matches the "Approved" date in the policy record's activity log.
- `review_cycle_days` in the lookup matches the governance committee's stated cadence for that policy.

(c) **Lookup join test**: run `| makeresults | eval record_key="POL-0001" | lookup nis2_is_security_policies policy_id AS record_key OUTPUT policy_name review_cycle_days | table record_key policy_name review_cycle_days`. Verify values match the CSV. If fields are null, the `policy_id` column header in the CSV may have a BOM character or trailing whitespace — open in a hex editor and verify.

(d) **Breach condition coverage test**: create synthetic events that trigger each of the four breach conditions:
- Condition 1: `risk_status=open`, `next_review_date` = 60 days ago. Expected: `review_breach=1`.
- Condition 2: `risk_score=85`, `risk_status=assessing`. Expected: `review_breach=1`.
- Condition 3: `approval_status=draft`. Expected: `review_breach=1`.
- Condition 4: `approval_status=approved`, `last_approved` = 400 days ago, `review_cycle_days=365`. Expected: `review_breach=1`, `days_since_approval=400`.
For each synthetic event, run the SPL and confirm the expected row appears.

(e) **Negative test**: modify the synthetic events so none trips a breach (risk_score=30, status=treated, future review date, approved policy within cycle). Run the search. Confirm zero results.

(f) **Freshness check**: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk | stats max(_indextime) as last_indexed | eval lag=now()-last_indexed | eval lag_hours=round(lag/3600,1) | table lag_hours`. If `lag_hours > 2`, the modular input is stalled. Similarly for `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy`.

### Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Risk and Policy Evidence`):
- **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Open risk breaches` (red ≥ 1, amber = 0) with drilldown to the filtered table. Single-value `Policy approval gaps` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Mean days since last policy approval` (amber ≥ 180, red ≥ 365). Single-value `Highest open risk score`.
- **Row 2 — Dual-lane breakdown**: Column chart with `source_lane` on the x-axis and stacked `review_breach` conditions. This shows whether the majority of breaches are from the risk register or policy register — useful for directing governance attention.
- **Row 3 — Risk score heatmap**: heatmap of `risk_score` by `record_key` over the last 90 days (using `timechart` on `index=grc`). Hot cells indicate persistent high scores that are not being treated.
- **Row 4 — Policy aging table**: sortable table of all in-scope policies with `policy_name`, `approval_status`, `days_since_approved`, `review_cycle_days`, and a calculated `overdue_days = days_since_approved - review_cycle_days`. Positive values are highlighted red.
- **Row 5 — Audit export**: button triggering `| outputlookup nis2_board_pack_extract.csv` for quarterly evidence packaging.

Runbook (owner: GRC / Risk Committee):
1. Monday 07:00: review alert email. Triage each row by `source_lane`:
   - `risk_register`: contact the `owner` field. Determine if the risk review was simply delayed (reschedule) or the risk requires escalation (treatment plan overdue). Update `next_review_date` in the GRC tool after the review.
   - `policy_record`: contact the policy owner. Determine if the policy is genuinely stalled (e.g., legal review pending) or the approval was completed but not recorded in ServiceNow. If the latter, update `u_last_approved` in ServiceNow and the `nis2_is_security_policies.csv` lookup.
2. For `risk_score ≥ 90`: escalate to the CISO within 24 hours. Document the escalation in `nis2_risk_exception_register.csv` if the risk is being formally accepted.
3. For `approval_status=rejected`: this is a governance failure. The policy was submitted for approval and rejected — the policy owner must address the rejection reasons and resubmit. This should be tracked as a finding in the internal audit log.
4. Quarterly: archive the dashboard PDF and CSV exports with RFC3161 timestamping via `evidenceSigning`. The signed bundle becomes part of the NIS2 compliance evidence pack.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting

- **Empty `index=grc` — zero risk events.** The ServiceNow modular input is disabled or the GRC module is not licensed. Check: Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow. If the input exists but shows "disabled", enable it and restart. If the GRC module is not licensed in ServiceNow, the `sn_grc_risk` table will return 403 errors — check `ta_snow_ticket.log` for `HTTP 403` or `Table not found` messages. Alternative: configure a scripted input that queries a different GRC tool's API and writes to `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk` via HEC.

- **All policies show `review_breach=1` — every policy is flagged.** Two common causes: (1) `review_cycle_days` is null in the CSV, causing `days_since_approval > null` to evaluate as false, but the `match(lower(approval_status),"draft|pending|rejected")` condition catches everything because ServiceNow's numeric states were not translated. Fix: add the `EVAL-approval_status` calculated field in `props.conf`. (2) The `last_approved` field format doesn't match `%Y-%m-%d`, so `strptime` returns null, making `days_since_approval` null. Fix: check `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy | head 5 | table last_approved` and adjust the `strptime` format string (e.g., `%d/%m/%Y` for European date formats, `%m/%d/%Y` for US formats).

- **`strptime` returns null for `last_approved`.** ServiceNow may return ISO 8601 with time (`2025-06-15 14:30:00`) while the SPL expects date-only (`%Y-%m-%d`). Fix: use `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S` or `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S` depending on the exact format. Test with: `| makeresults | eval last_approved="2025-06-15 14:30:00" | eval e=strptime(last_approved,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | table e`.

- **Join produces duplicate rows after ServiceNow upgrade.** ServiceNow upgrades can change field names (e.g., `state` becomes `approval_status` natively). If both the raw field and the FIELDALIAS exist, `coalesce` may pick the wrong one. Fix: after any ServiceNow upgrade, run `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy latest | head 5 | table _raw` and verify field names. Update `props.conf` aliases as needed. Remove duplicate aliases.

- **Risk score scale mismatch — all scores below threshold.** Your GRC tool uses a 1-5 scale but the SPL tests `risk_score >= 75`. Fix: normalize in `props.conf` with `EVAL-risk_score = case(risk_rating=5, 100, risk_rating=4, 80, risk_rating=3, 60, risk_rating=2, 40, risk_rating=1, 20)`. Document the mapping in the runbook. Alternatively, adjust the threshold: `| eval review_breach=if(source_lane="risk_register" AND risk_score>=4 AND match(...))` if keeping the native scale.

- **Lookup key drift after CMDB merge or GRC migration.** After merging two ServiceNow instances or migrating from Archer to ServiceNow, `risk_id` or `policy_number` values change. The `nis2_is_security_policies.csv` lookup still contains old keys. Fix: run `index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk | stats dc(risk_id) as new_ids | appendpipe [| inputlookup nis2_is_security_policies.csv | stats dc(policy_id) as csv_ids] | stats values(*) as *` to compare unique key counts. Rebuild the CSV from the new system of record. During the transition, maintain a mapping CSV `nis2_key_migration.csv` with `old_id, new_id` and apply before the main lookup.

- **Time zone skew between ServiceNow and Splunk.** ServiceNow stores dates in UTC by default, but the user's ServiceNow UI may display in local time. If the TA pulls the display value instead of the internal value, `next_review_date` and `last_approved` may be off by hours. For date-only fields this rarely matters, but for `strptime` it can cause null returns if the format includes a timezone offset. Fix: ensure the TA's `sysparm_display_value=false` setting is enabled (this returns internal UTC values). Check: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy | head 1 | table u_last_approved` — if it shows "15/06/2025" instead of "2025-06-15", the TA is returning display values.

- **API rate limiting from ServiceNow.** ServiceNow enforces rate limits (default: 100 requests per minute for REST API). If the TA pulls multiple tables with short intervals, it may hit `429 Too Many Requests`. Fix: stagger input intervals (e.g., `sn_grc_policy` at minute 0, `sn_grc_risk` at minute 15). Monitor `ta_snow_ticket.log` for `429` errors. If persistent, request a rate limit increase from your ServiceNow administrator or use the TA's batch mode with `sysparm_limit=1000` per request.



## SPL

```spl
(index=grc sourcetype=grc:risk earliest=-730d@d) OR (index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy earliest=-730d@d)
| eval record_key=coalesce(risk_id,policy_number,u_policy_id)
| eval source_lane=if(index="grc","risk_register","policy_record")
| lookup nis2_is_security_policies policy_id AS record_key OUTPUT policy_name, approval_status, last_approved, review_cycle_days
| eval approved_epoch=if(isnotnull(last_approved),strptime(last_approved,"%Y-%m-%d"),null())
| eval days_since_approval=if(isnotnull(approved_epoch),round((now()-approved_epoch)/86400,1),null())
| eval next_epoch=if(isnotnull(next_review_date),strptime(next_review_date,"%Y-%m-%d"),null())
| eval review_breach=0
| eval review_breach=if(source_lane="risk_register" AND isnotnull(next_epoch) AND now()>next_epoch,1,review_breach)
| eval review_breach=if(source_lane="risk_register" AND risk_score>=75 AND match(lower(risk_status),"open|new|assessing"),1,review_breach)
| eval review_breach=if(source_lane="policy_record" AND match(lower(approval_status),"draft|pending|rejected"),1,review_breach)
| eval review_breach=if(source_lane="policy_record" AND isnotnull(days_since_approval) AND days_since_approval>review_cycle_days,1,review_breach)
| where review_breach=1
| stats latest(risk_score) as risk_score latest(approval_status) as approval_status latest(days_since_approval) as days_since_approved latest(policy_name) as policy_name by record_key source_lane
| sort 0 - risk_score
```

## Visualization

Dashboard Studio: matrix by source_lane; bar chart of aging `days_since_approved`; risk score trend via overlay `timechart` on `index=grc` subset only.

## Known False Positives

**Quarterly risk workshop holds scores open on purpose:** Distinguish tickets tagged `workshop_hold` in the source system. Suppress with `lookup nis2_risk_exception_register risk_id OUTPUT suppress_until | where now()>suppress_until OR isnull(suppress_until)`.

**Policy translations pending legal review:** Distinguish `approval_status=pending` with `u_locale` not equal to master language. Suppress via `where NOT (approval_status="pending" AND u_locale!="en")` until legal signs off.

**ServiceNow clone noise:** Distinguish `sys_created_by=clone` or `instance=dev`. Suppress using `where instance="prod"` after indexing `instance` from the TA configuration.

**Lookup key drift after CMDB merge:** Distinguish blank `record_key` after merge events. Suppress nothing; fix CSV keys and use the exception register until remapped.

**Duplicate backlog replays:** Distinguish `_cd` duplication during indexer recovery. Suppress with `| dedup record_key source_lane` in the alert-only variant.

- [Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg_impl/2024/2690/oj)
